MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar May-13-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, May 13, 2025, in Department 304, Line 4.
No appearances, not contested, The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows:

3. Defendant Schneider Electric USA, Inc.'s (Formerly Known as Square D Company) ("Defendant") Motion for Summary Judgment, or in the Alternative, Summary Adjudication is DENIED. Opposition filed. Reply filed.
Defendant failed to demonstrate that it propounded comprehensive discovery designed to elicit from Plaintiff all facts, witnesses, and documents supporting his claim. (See Defendant's Index of Exhibits.) Instead, Defendant attached Plaintiff's deposition testimony as its sole affirmative evidence. (Id.) However, deposition testimony indicating lack of knowledge of facts to support a claim alone does not meet the initial burden of proof on a defendant's motion for summary judgment. (Andrews v. Foster Wheeler LLC (2006) 138 Cal.App.4th 96, 103; Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1439-40.)
Although Defendant later served its own "state all facts" special interrogatories, it did not submit Plaintiff's responses in its moving papers, but instead attached them for the first time in its reply brief. (Reply, 2-3.) Defendant asserts that it was unable to include these responses earlier because Plaintiff's responses to the propounded discovery were due after its motion was filed. (Id. at 1.) Defendant does not explain why it did not serve discovery before the Court granted Plaintiff's preference motion. Regardless, the timing does not excuse the procedural requirement that a moving party may not rely on evidence introduced for the first time on reply to meet its initial burden. (Jay v. Mahaffey (2013) 218 Cal.App.4th 1522, 1537-1538; San Diego Watercrafts, Inc. v. Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. (2002) 102 Cal.App.4th 308, 316.) Based on the evidence presented, the Court cannot infer that Plaintiff will not be able to meet his evidentiary burden regarding exposure. (See Scheiding v. Dinwiddie Constr. Co. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 64, 81 [stating that "[c]ourts can infer nothing at all with respect to questions which were neither asked nor answered"].) Consequently, Defendant failed to meet its initial burden of proof, and its motion must be denied.
Moreover, Defendant's Motion for Summary Adjudication as to Plaintiff's claim for punitive damages is DENIED. As stated above, Plaintiff's deposition testimony is not enough by itself to determine whether Defendant acted with malice, oppression, or fraud in failing to disclose asbestos health hazards.

The Court to issue order. Judge: Ethan P. Schulman. Clerk: Johnny Sengmany. Reporter: NOT REPORTED (304/EPS).

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Preference Pursuant To C.C.P. 36(A) And 36(D)
Off Calendar Feb-04-2025 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances, not contested, and the tentative ruling is adopted as follows:

The Court GRANTS Plaintiffs' Motion for Order Granting Preference in Setting Case for Trial, and Extending Discovery Cutoff under C.C.P. Section 36 (a) and (d). Oppositions filed. Joinders filed. Reply filed. Plaintiff provided clear and convincing medical documentation from Dr. Ripley and Dr. Doyle that Mr. Darro suffers from incurable malignant pleural mesothelioma, raising substantial medical doubt of his survival beyond six months. The court further finds that Mr. Darro's health is such that the interests of justice will be served by granting this preference.

Defendants' oppositions and conditional demands are meritless. Defendants in their oppositions request that the court set the trial date no sooner than 120 days from the hearing and impose certain conditions on discovery and pre-trial motions. However, the court already adheres to the practice of scheduling trial dates as close to 120 days as possible. Moreover, the court will not impose conditions on Plaintiffs' right to a preferential trial date. "Where a party meets the requisite standard for calendar preference under [Code of Civil Procedure section 36] subdivision (a), preference must be granted. No weighing of interests is involved." (Fox v. Superior Court (2018) 21 Cal.App.5th 529, 535; see also, e.g., Miller v. Superior Court (1990) 221 Cal.App.3d 1200, 1204 [statute "grants a mandatory and absolute right to trial preference"]; Swaithes v. Superior Court (1989) 212 Cal.App.3d 1082, 1085 [trial court "has no power to balance the different interests of opposing litigants in applying the provision"]; Koch-Ash v. Superior Court (1986) 180 Cal.App.3d 689, 694 [section 36(a) "must be deemed to be mandatory and absolute" and "no discretion is left to trial courts."].)

1. The trial date is May 19, 2025, at 11:15 a.m. in Department 206.
a. Last day pursuant to C.C.P. section 36(f) is June 4, 2025.
b. The parties shall follow the California Rules of Court, San Francisco Local Rules, and Local Rule 20.

2. The last day for hearing summary judgment/adjudication motions is May 13, 2025.
a. Summary judgment/adjudication motions shall be brought on regular notice pursuant to the relevant provisions of the C.C.P., unless the parties stipulate otherwise.
b. Before a party files and serves a summary judgment/adjudication motion, it must contact the clerk to make a reservation.
c. The Court allows a maximum of four summary judgment/adjudication motions per day to be calendared, unless good cause is found to exceed this number. Contact the clerk to schedule a good cause hearing.

3. Time to respond to written discovery not yet served is shortened to 20 days.
a. For written discovery that has already been served, responses are due within 20 days of this hearing or by the date determined by the C.C.P., whichever is earlier.
b. Any issue/dispute that requires meet and confer, shall occur in person or via telephone, not by email or letter.

4. Electronic service is considered the equivalent of personal service.
5. The fact discovery cut-off date is May 2, 2025.
6. The expert discovery cut-off date is May 16, 2025.

7. All bankruptcy documents shall be turned over to defendants no later than March 6, 2025.
a. If Plaintiff submits documents to bankruptcy trusts after this date, they must notify Defendants no later than five days after submission.
b. The fact discovery cut-off does not apply to bankruptcy documents. (Mini minutes continue in the next entry)
